findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is single or multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions, frequently located at the corticomedullary junction or within the deep gray matter such as the basal ganglia. In the context of an immunocompromised host, these lesions typically demonstrate thin, smooth ring or nodular enhancement following contrast administration.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a substantial amount of surrounding vasogenic oedema and mass effect on adjacent structures. On MRI, there may be central T2 hyperintensity with an isointense to hypointense capsule, though the profound central restricted diffusion typical of pyogenic abscesses is typically absent.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an incomplete, open-ring enhancement pattern pointing toward an active demyelinating plaque. Furthermore, there is no thick, irregular, nodular enhancement crossing the corpus callosum to suggest a glioblastoma multiforme.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of multiple ring-enhancing lesions localising to the basal ganglia and centrum semiovale in an immunocompromised patient.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Toxoplasmosis abscess, given the multiplicity of ring-enhancing lesions targeting the deep gray matter. This opportunistic parasitic infection is the primary consideration in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with advanced HIV, presenting with these characteristic imaging findings.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Neoplasm, however primary central nervous system lymphoma typically demonstrates a thicker, more nodular rim of enhancement and frequently involves the subependymal surfaces. Furthermore, glioblastoma multiforme or metastatic disease usually presents with a thicker, more irregular rind of enhancement rather than a thin, smooth capsule.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Demyelinating disease, given that tumefactive demyelination can present as a large ring-enhancing mass with surrounding edema, and missing this could lead to inappropriate antimicrobial treatment rather than necessary high-dose steroids. However, demyelinating plaques characteristically exhibit incomplete or 'open-ring' enhancement directed toward the cortex, which is absent here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the extent of mass effect, midline shift, and any impending cerebral herniation requiring urgent neurosurgical intervention.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is highly useful to evaluate the response to an empiric trial of anti-toxoplasmosis therapy, as a rapid reduction in lesion size and enhancement over 10 to 14 days strongly confirms the diagnosis without the need for biopsy.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the enhancement pattern, assessing for restricted diffusion to differentiate from pyogenic abscesses, and detecting subtle occult lesions.
management.mriSequences: I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and ADC maps, as toxoplasmosis typically lacks the profound central restricted diffusion characteristic of pyogenic abscesses, alongside MR spectroscopy which may help differentiate it from lymphoma by demonstrating an elevated lipid/lactate peak and decreased choline.
management.nuclearMedicine: A thallium SPECT or 18F-FDG PET scan could be highly beneficial if the diagnosis remains equivocal, as toxoplasmosis typically shows decreased uptake compared to the markedly increased radiotracer uptake seen in primary CNS lymphoma.
management.intervention: If the patient fails to respond to an empiric trial of anti-toxoplasmosis therapy and the diagnosis remains in doubt, I would specify an image-guided stereotactic brain biopsy targeting the enhancing rim to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the number and location of the lesions, the severity of any associated mass effect, and the specific diffusion characteristics to guide immediate empiric antimicrobial therapy versus urgent biopsy.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is single or multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions, frequently located at the corticomedullary junction or within the deep gray matter such as the basal ganglia. In the context of an immunocompromised host, these lesions typically demonstrate thin, smooth ring or nodular enhancement following contrast administration. Supporting features include a substantial amount of surrounding vasogenic oedema and mass effect on adjacent structures. On MRI, there may be central T2 hyperintensity with an isointense to hypointense capsule, though the profound central restricted diffusion typical of pyogenic abscesses is typically absent. Importantly, there is no evidence of an incomplete, open-ring enhancement pattern pointing toward an active demyelinating plaque. Furthermore, there is no thick, irregular, nodular enhancement crossing the corpus callosum to suggest a glioblastoma multiforme. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of multiple ring-enhancing lesions localising to the basal ganglia and centrum semiovale in an immunocompromised patient.

The appearances are most in keeping with Toxoplasmosis abscess, given the multiplicity of ring-enhancing lesions targeting the deep gray matter. This opportunistic parasitic infection is the primary consideration in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with advanced HIV, presenting with these characteristic imaging findings. The principle differential would be Neoplasm, however primary central nervous system lymphoma typically demonstrates a thicker, more nodular rim of enhancement and frequently involves the subependymal surfaces. Furthermore, glioblastoma multiforme or metastatic disease usually presents with a thicker, more irregular rind of enhancement rather than a thin, smooth capsule. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Demyelinating disease, given that tumefactive demyelination can present as a large ring-enhancing mass with surrounding edema, and missing this could lead to inappropriate antimicrobial treatment rather than necessary high-dose steroids. However, demyelinating plaques characteristically exhibit incomplete or 'open-ring' enhancement directed toward the cortex, which is absent here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the extent of mass effect, midline shift, and any impending cerebral herniation requiring urgent neurosurgical intervention. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is highly useful to evaluate the response to an empiric trial of anti-toxoplasmosis therapy, as a rapid reduction in lesion size and enhancement over 10 to 14 days strongly confirms the diagnosis without the need for biopsy. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the enhancement pattern, assessing for restricted diffusion to differentiate from pyogenic abscesses, and detecting subtle occult lesions. I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and ADC maps, as toxoplasmosis typically lacks the profound central restricted diffusion characteristic of pyogenic abscesses, alongside MR spectroscopy which may help differentiate it from lymphoma by demonstrating an elevated lipid/lactate peak and decreased choline. A thallium SPECT or 18F-FDG PET scan could be highly beneficial if the diagnosis remains equivocal, as toxoplasmosis typically shows decreased uptake compared to the markedly increased radiotracer uptake seen in primary CNS lymphoma. If the patient fails to respond to an empiric trial of anti-toxoplasmosis therapy and the diagnosis remains in doubt, I would specify an image-guided stereotactic brain biopsy targeting the enhancing rim to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis. This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the number and location of the lesions, the severity of any associated mass effect, and the specific diffusion characteristics to guide immediate empiric antimicrobial therapy versus urgent biopsy.
